schools.summary: The letter to Sardis (old capital of Lydia, home of Croesus) — Christ 'who hath the seven Spirits and seven stars' (the fulness of the Spirit and the messengers, rebuking a church lacking the Spirit's life); 'thou hast a name that thou livest, and art dead' (a fair reputation and goodly outward show, passing among men as living, but spiritually dead in Christ's sight); the peril not heresy or persecution but deadness hidden under a respectable name; the Spirit seeing the inward death (Wesley).
Victorinus: this class sets forth men 'Christians only in name.' Bede: the bishop reproved for not diligently correcting the bad.

The call to wakefulness — 'be watchful' (ginou gregoron, become wakeful, a settled habit of vigilance against the drowsy condition); 'strengthen the things which remain, that are ready to die' (a flickering remnant of true piety — knowledge, good desires, convictions — at the point of death, to be braced before it expires); 'I have not found thy works perfect before God' (not complete or full, outwardly busy but inwardly hollow); the mercy — not yet wholly dead, something to strengthen, time to do it.

The chain of commands — 'remember how thou hast received and heard' (call to mind the truth received as a permanent deposit and the joy and zeal of first hearing, the spur to recovery); 'hold fast, and repent' (hold the grace remaining, turn back from the deadness); the warning 'if thou shalt not watch, I will come on thee as a thief' (suddenly, unexpectedly, in judgment, Mt 24:43; the careless church overtaken before aware); the thief-like coming reserved for the unwatchful, vigilance the safeguard.

themes: I will come as a thief

schools.summary: The faithful few — even in so dead a church 'thou hast a few names' (a few persons) 'which have not defiled their garments' (a character unstained by the prevailing deadness, the loving witness not overlooking the good, Ellicott); 'they shall walk with me in white' (white the colour of purity, victory, festal joy; intimate fellowship in the robes of the redeemed); 'for they are worthy' (counted worthy through grace, fit companions for the holy Christ); God always preserving a remnant.

The threefold promise to the overcomer — 'clothed in white raiment' (the robe of purity, victory, joy, extended from the faithful few to every conqueror); 'I will not blot out his name out of the book of life' (the figure from civic/church registers; the name permanently enrolled, never erased, secure against the dead church's empty name); 'I will confess his name before my Father and his angels' (Christ owning the faithful in heaven's assembly, as he promised in the Gospels); answering Sardis's deadness, empty name, and shame.

themes: confess his name before my Father

schools.summary: The standing call closing the letter — 'he that hath an ear, let him hear' (an appeal to the spiritually attentive to listen and lay it to heart); 'what the Spirit saith unto the churches' (Christ's words the Spirit's words, addressed to one church but for all, each taking warning and comfort from what is said to every other); the formula standing before the promise in the first three letters, after it in the last three, dividing the seven; the universal relevance of these messages.

The titles of the holy and true Lord — 'he that is holy, he that is true' (holiness essentially dwelling in him, the fountain of truth who cannot lie; divine titles claimed by Christ); 'he that hath the key of David' (Isa 22:22, the token of a prince's authority to open and shut every door of his house, the royal messianic authority over the kingdom of God, Wesley); 'openeth and no man shutteth, shutteth and no man openeth' (his decisions final and irreversible); the Lord of the keys setting open doors before a weak church.

themes: he that is holy, he that is true

schools.summary: The open door set before the weak but faithful church — 'I have set before thee an open door, and no man can shut it' (the Lord of the keys opening a door no enemy can close, chiefly of gospel opportunity, 1 Cor 16:9; Col 4:3, some adding entrance to the kingdom); the threefold ground — 'a little strength' (small, weak, a despised company, yet not despised by Christ), 'hast kept my word' (held the truth despite weakness), 'hast not denied my name' (confessed Christ under pressure); not strength but faithfulness rewarded.

themes: an open door, and no man can shut it

schools.summary: The humbling of false adversaries — those 'of the synagogue of Satan, which say they are Jews and are not' (opponents claiming to be God's people but, rejecting Christ and persecuting the church, belonging to Satan, as at Smyrna, Rev 2:9); Christ will 'make them come and worship before thy feet' (the proud brought low, by conversion — 'I give some,' Ellicott — or forced acknowledgment); 'to know that I have loved thee' (made to recognize the scorned church as the object of Christ's special love); the tables turned, the despised vindicated.

themes: know that I have loved thee

schools.summary: The promise of preservation — the ground 'thou hast kept the word of my patience' (held Christ's gospel that calls for patient endurance, steadfast under trial); the answering 'I also will keep thee' (a pointed play — the one who keeps God's word is himself kept, the gracious requital of the kingdom, Ellicott); 'the hour of temptation which shall come upon all the world' (a season of severe widespread testing of earth's inhabitants); the debated nuance of 'keep from' — kept out of the trial, or kept safe through it (Ellicott), the faithful guarded either way.

debates.question: Does 'keep thee from the hour of temptation' mean removal from or preservation through the trial?

sides.position: kept safe through and in the midst of the trial (the one who keeps God's word is kept)
kept from the hour so as not to enter the temptation as the careless do

schools.summary: The charge to hold fast the crown — 'behold, I come quickly' (the keynote of the book, Christ's coming near and certain, the expectation nerving the church to endure); 'hold that fast which thou hast' (keep a firm grip on the faith and grace possessed, keep on keeping his word); 'that no man take thy crown' (the crown of life appointed for the faithful, lost by failing to persevere, another seizing the prize they let slip); the race for a garland (1 Cor 9:24, Ellicott), not slackening near the goal; perseverance securing the prize.

themes: hold that fast which thou hast

schools.summary: The promise of permanence and belonging — 'a pillar in the temple of my God' (a column combining stability and honour, Gal 2:9; some recalling Jachin and Boaz; an unshaken pillar for a city shaken by earthquakes, Ellicott); 'he shall go out no more' (fixed forever in God's presence); the threefold inscription — 'the name of my God' (God's own possession), 'the name of the city... new Jerusalem' (citizen of the heavenly city), Christ's 'new name' (belonging to the exalted Christ); ownership, citizenship, intimate belonging.

themes: a pillar in the temple of my God
he shall go out no more
new Jerusalem and my new name

schools.summary: The closing call to Philadelphia — 'he that hath an ear, let him hear' (the appeal to every attentive soul to lay to heart); 'what the Spirit saith unto the churches' (through Philadelphia to all — the open door, preservation, crown, pillar, and names held out to every faithful church); the call here standing after the promise, as in the last four letters (before it in the first three), dividing the seven; the reward lingering as the final word; the universal, abiding relevance of these messages.

The titles of the Amen — 'the Amen' (from aman, firm/faithful/true; Christ the embodiment of certainty, the divine Amen in whom God's promises are sure); 'the faithful and true witness' (whose testimony never fails, fitting a church of self-deceiving falsehood, Rev 1:5); 'the beginning of the creation of God' — not the first creature made (an error rejected) but the Author, Prince, Head, and Ruler of creation, its originating source (Clarke, Wesley); fitting a church that boasts falsely, addressed by the true Witness and Lord of creation.

debates.question: Does 'the beginning of the creation of God' mean Christ was the first creature?

sides.position: no — it means the Author, Head, Prince, and Ruler of creation, its originating source, not its first product

themes: the beginning of the creation of God

schools.summary: The rebuke of indifference — 'neither cold nor hot' (Laodicea neither frigidly irreligious nor fervently devoted but listless, neither heathen nor truly Christian, Clarke); 'I would thou wert cold or hot' (Christ preferring either extreme to the middle state); 'cold' (no pretence of religion) and 'hot' (zestos, boiling, fervent and zealous); the paradox that coldness is preferred to lukewarmness — the self-satisfied half-hearted professor harder to reach and more nauseating than the honest unbeliever; the danger of a comfortable, prosperous church.

themes: I would thou wert cold or hot

schools.summary: Christ's loathing of lukewarmness — 'because thou art lukewarm' (chliaros, tepid, the cause of the sentence) 'I will spue thee out of my mouth' (the well-known nauseating effect of tepid water, conveying Christ's intense disgust and determination to reject the half-hearted utterly); lukewarm religion, which men think respectable, nauseating to Christ; not the cold sinner but the tepid professor drawing this revulsion; the threat not yet executed — 'unless thou repent' (Wesley), the door of repentance open, meant to shock the complacent.

themes: I will spue thee out of my mouth

schools.summary: The self-deception of the Laodiceans — the boast 'I am rich, and increased with goods, and have need of nothing' (reckoning themselves spiritually wealthy and self-sufficient, the pride of their wealthy city infecting their religion; complacency breeding the lukewarm state); Christ's contradiction 'knowest not' (their delusion total, blind to their condition); the fivefold verdict piercing each boast — 'wretched, miserable, poor, blind, and naked'; the false conviction of safety cutting the nerves of diligence (Clarke); the peril of the prosperous church.

themes: I am rich... have need of nothing

schools.summary: The counsel to buy the true riches — a tender irony, 'I counsel thee to buy' what their boasted wealth could not purchase, yet 'without money' (Isa 55:1), received freely from Christ; the threefold remedy answering their destitution — 'gold tried in the fire' (against poverty: true living faith refined in affliction), 'white raiment' (against nakedness: the robe of Christ's righteousness and holiness), 'eyesalve' (against blindness: the Spirit's anointing giving spiritual sight); all only 'of me' — the church that needed nothing shown it needs everything, all in him.

themes: buy of me gold tried in the fire

schools.summary: Rebuke the proof of love — 'as many as I love, I rebuke and chasten' (a free rendering of Prov 3:12; Heb 12:6; Christ's reproof not the contrary of love but its proof, as a father corrects beloved children); the emphatic 'I'; the two words 'rebuke' (conscience-piercing conviction, the Spirit's word) and 'chasten' (to discipline, educate by correction); the tender wonder — the church threatened with spewing assured of his love; the charge 'be zealous and repent' (against lukewarmness, be fervent and turn).

themes: as many as I love, I rebuke and chasten

schools.summary: Christ knocking at the door — 'I stand at the door, and knock' (hesteka, perfect; the Lord who might have cast them out instead patiently seeking entrance, the way back not shut, Barnes; true of his advent, Jas 5:9, and the individual response, Lu 12:36); 'if any man hear my voice, and open' (the appeal to each single soul, the knock his word and providence, the opening faith's willing reception; the door opened from within, Christ not forcing the latch); 'sup with him, and he with me' (the shared meal, closest communion); grace knocking at the worst of the seven churches.

themes: I stand at the door, and knock
if any man open the door

schools.summary: The promise of sharing Christ's throne — 'to him that overcometh will I grant to sit with me in my throne' (the conqueror exalted to share Christ's royal rule and glory, reigning with the King, Rom 8:17); the pattern in Christ's own experience 'even as I also overcame, and am set down with my Father in his throne' (Christ first overcoming and enthroned, the overcomer following from conflict to coronation); two thrones — Christ sharing the Father's by right, the saints sharing Christ's by grace as joint-heirs; the loftiest promise, given to the worst church.

themes: sit with me in my throne

schools.summary: The final call to the seven churches — 'he that hath an ear, let him hear' (the last of seven summons, pressing the duty of heeding); 'what the Spirit saith unto the churches' (the close of all seven, a call to every church to lay the whole series to heart); the structural note (Wesley via Clarke) — the call standing before the promise in the first three letters, after it in the last four, dividing the seven; the refrain binding the letters and asserting their abiding relevance to the whole church in every age; the letters ending as the door to the throne-vision opens.
